text:
This study aims to investigate the Madurese society’s individual and collective attitudes toward *carok* (violent ritual duels) and what triggers or motivates this tradition to continue to exist. This study uses a descriptive phenomenological method. The researcher collects the data by observing, questioning, and interviewing 195 respondents in four regencies in Madura.

Based on the survey data, the stereotypes about the Madurese people, portrayed negatively in the mass media, are largely unfounded. Although each respondent, when interviewed individually, reacted negatively to *carok*, indicating that 75% of them felt no pride for *carok*, 60% said they would not carry out *carok* under any circumstances, and 77.05% said they supported the peaceful settlement of conflicts through means that were in line with Islam and the law. When asked collectively as a community, 86.11% stated that *carok* was not part of Madurese culture, and 81.11% said the community was a peaceful community. The various factors that may trigger the occurrence of *carok* have also been identified. They include: gender and honour issues, as well as misunderstandings, disputes over inheritance, religious matters, theft and debt. The authors’ view is that *carok* may sometimes be carried out as a means to defend one’s honour on an individual basis, but the occurrence of *carok* is decreasing, and society is progressing towards the promotion of education and Islam, thus making the resolution of conflicts through legal means, and in a peaceful manner, the prevailing over the retention of *carok*.
